default_negative_review.method: Exhaustive endpoint-table review under the direct-support rule; every unlisted eligible pair was reviewed as NO_IN_SCHEMA_CONNECTION before model execution.
default_negative_review.boundary: Broad topical similarity, transitive links through an intermediate object, and unsupported world knowledge do not create an edge.

evidence.span: The first-order Taylor approximation uses the function value and gradient at \(x\) to approximate the value after a small displacement \(\Delta x\). It is formalized by the first-order Taylor formula

\[
T_1(x+\Delta x;x)=f(x)+\nabla f(x)^\top\Delta x.
\]

pairs.rationale: The first-order Taylor Formula contains the gradient as its required linear term.

evidence.span: Gradient descent requires the gradient of the objective function. With step size \(\alpha_k>0\), the method moves opposite to the gradient. The gradient descent update is

\[
x_{k+1}=x_k-\alpha_k\nabla f(x_k).
\]

pairs.rationale: Gradient Descent explicitly uses the gradient to define its descent direction.

pairs.rationale: The Gradient Descent Update cannot be stated without the gradient term.

evidence.span: A statistical model is a family of probability distributions indexed by an unknown parameter \(\theta\). For observed data, the log-likelihood measures how the assumed parameter value supports those observations.

Maximum likelihood estimation treats the log-likelihood as an objective function and selects a parameter value that maximizes it. The score function is the gradient of the log-likelihood with respect to the parameter,

pairs.rationale: Maximum Likelihood Estimation requires a Statistical Model from which its Log-Likelihood is defined.

evidence.span: A statistical model is a family of probability distributions indexed by an unknown parameter \(\theta\). For observed data, the log-likelihood measures how the assumed parameter value supports those observations.

For a Gaussian linear regression model with constant variance, maximizing the log-likelihood is equivalent to minimizing the least squares objective. Under this assumption, ordinary least squares and maximum likelihood estimation select the same coefficient vector.

pairs.rationale: A Gaussian Linear Regression model is an instance of a Statistical Model, a relation absent from ADR-004.

evidence.span: Maximum likelihood estimation treats the log-likelihood as an objective function and selects a parameter value that maximizes it. The score function is the gradient of the log-likelihood with respect to the parameter,

\[
s(\theta)=\nabla_{\theta}\ell(\theta).
\]

At an interior differentiable maximum, a candidate satisfies the score equation \(s(\theta)=0\). Finding such a candidate is a root-finding problem. When the score equation has no closed-form solution, Newton's root-finding method can be applied within maximum likelihood estimation.

pairs.rationale: The Score Function is used to form the stationarity condition for an MLE candidate.

evidence.span: Maximum likelihood estimation treats the log-likelihood as an objective function and selects a parameter value that maximizes it. The score function is the gradient of the log-likelihood with respect to the parameter,

\[

At an interior differentiable maximum, a candidate satisfies the score equation \(s(\theta)=0\). Finding such a candidate is a root-finding problem. When the score equation has no closed-form solution, Newton's root-finding method can be applied within maximum likelihood estimation.

pairs.rationale: The Score Equation supplies the stationarity condition used to identify an MLE candidate.

evidence.span: For a Gaussian linear regression model with constant variance, maximizing the log-likelihood is equivalent to minimizing the least squares objective. Under this assumption, ordinary least squares and maximum likelihood estimation select the same coefficient vector.

pairs.rationale: Maximum Likelihood Estimation is applied to the Gaussian Linear Regression model described in the lecture.

evidence.span: For a Gaussian linear regression model with constant variance, maximizing the log-likelihood is equivalent to minimizing the least squares objective. Under this assumption, ordinary least squares and maximum likelihood estimation select the same coefficient vector.

Under a Gaussian noise model, ordinary least squares also has a maximum likelihood estimation interpretation. That equivalence depends on the statistical assumptions and is not an identity between the two methods in every model.

pairs.rationale: Ordinary Least Squares and Maximum Likelihood Estimation coincide only under the stated Gaussian assumptions.

evidence.span: For observations \((x_i,y_i)\), the mean squared error formula is

\[
\operatorname{MSE}(\beta)=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i-x_i^\top\beta)^2.
\]
This formula formalizes the least squares objective up to a positive constant scaling. Gradient descent can be applied to ordinary least squares by using the gradient of this objective function.

pairs.rationale: The MSE Formula explicitly formalizes the Least Squares Objective up to positive scaling.

evidence.span: Linear regression models a response using a linear function of the input features. Ordinary least squares fits a linear regression model by minimizing the least squares objective over the coefficient vector \(\beta\).

pairs.rationale: Ordinary Least Squares is defined by minimizing the Least Squares Objective.

evidence.span: Linear regression models a response using a linear function of the input features. Ordinary least squares fits a linear regression model by minimizing the least squares objective over the coefficient vector \(\beta\).

Ridge regression extends ordinary least squares by adding a squared-coefficient penalty to the objective. The penalty can improve numerical stability and reduce sensitivity to highly correlated features.

pairs.rationale: Ridge Regression retains the Least Squares Objective and adds a penalty to it.

evidence.span: The damped Newton method extends Newton's root-finding method by multiplying the correction by a factor \(\lambda_k\in(0,1]\). Damping can prevent a full Newton correction from moving too far when the current iterate is not yet near a root.

pairs.rationale: The Damped Newton Method explicitly extends Newton's method by scaling its correction.
